title: Electoral Pluralism Index
subtitle: Data by the [Economist Intelligence Unit](#dod:economist-intelligence-unit). Expert estimates of the extent to which all citizens can choose their political leaders in free and fair elections. Higher values indicate more pluralism.
caption: The 2024 Electoral Pluralism Index maps the extent to which citizens can choose political leaders in free and fair elections across the globe, using a color scale from 1 (lowest pluralism, dark red) to 10 (highest pluralism, dark blue). Countries in western and northern Europe — including Norway, Sweden, Germany, and the United Kingdom — fall within the highest band, shaded in deep blue tones corresponding to scores of 8 or above. Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and Japan also occupy this top tier. In contrast, large swaths of central Asia, the Middle East, and sub-Saharan Africa are colored in dark red, indicating scores at or near 1 — visible in Russia, China, Saudi Arabia, Iran, and much of the Sahel region including Mali, Niger, and Chad. Intermediate values appear across Latin America and parts of South and Southeast Asia: Brazil and India sit in the 5–6 range (light yellow to pale orange), while Mexico and Indonesia hover around 6–7 (pale blue). The United States and France register in the 7–8 band, shown as medium-light blue. Several countries — notably Greenland, Western Sahara, and North Korea — carry no data, marked with diagonal hatching. The geographic distribution shows a clear clustering: high pluralism concentrated in established democracies of the Global North and Oceania; low pluralism dominating authoritarian regimes and conflict-affected states; and moderate scores scattered across emerging democracies and hybrid regimes. The most visible pattern is the sharp continental divide between the deeply blue zones of Western Europe and Anglophone settler states versus the contiguous red belts stretching from Eastern Europe through Central Asia into the Horn of Africa.
